source_paper_title: Marginal Values of a Stochastic Game
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Attia_2025, title={Marginal Values of a Stochastic Game}, volume={50}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.0297}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.0297}, number={1}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Attia, Luc and Oliu-Barton, Miquel and Saona, Raimundo}, year={2025}, month=feb, pages={482–505} }
context:
Section 6.2 (Compact-continuous stochastic games), page 16 (in the provided 19-page text)

Determining necessary and sufficient conditions on \(I\), \(J\), \(g\), and \(q\) which ensure the convergence of the discounted values or the existence of the value is a problem. Bolte, Gaubert and Vigeral [5] provided sufficient conditions, namely that \(g\) and \(q\) are separable and definable. Without going into a precise definition of these two conditions, they hold in particular when the pay-off function \(g\) and the transition \(q\) are polynomials in the players’ actions. However, the case where \(I\), \(J\), \(g\), and \(q\) are semi-algebraic is still unsolved.
title: Characterize convergence of discounted values in compact-continuous zero-sum stochastic games
background_latex:
A two-player zero-sum stochastic game is specified by a finite state set \(K=\{1,\dots,n\}\), compact metric action sets \(I\) and \(J\), a bounded stage payoff (to Player 1) \(g:K\times I\times J\to \mathbb{R}\), and a transition kernel \(q:K\times I\times J\to \Delta(K)\), both continuous in \((i,j)\) (with \(\Delta(K)\) the simplex over \(K\)).

For a discount factor \(\lambda\in(0,1]\), the \(\lambda\)-discounted payoff starting from an initial state \(k\in K\) is
\[
\mathbb{E}\Big[\sum_{m\ge 1} \lambda(1-\lambda)^{m-1} g(k_m,i_m,j_m)\Big],
\]
where \(k_1=k\) and \(k_{m+1}\sim q(k_m,i_m,j_m)\). A (behavioral) strategy maps each player’s observation history to a probability distribution over actions; in these perfect-monitoring games, both players observe the current state and past actions.

Let \(v^k_\lambda\in\mathbb{R}\) denote the value of the \(\lambda\)-discounted game starting from state \(k\) (existence follows from standard minimax arguments under compactness/continuity). The family \((v^k_\lambda)_{\lambda\downarrow 0}\) is said to converge if \(\lim_{\lambda\to 0} v^k_\lambda\) exists for each \(k\in K\).

One is also interested in the (uniform) long-run value \(v^k\), defined via the ability of each player to guarantee, for all sufficiently large horizons \(M\), an expected average payoff over the first \(M\) stages within \(\varepsilon\) of \(v^k\). In the finite-action case, this value exists and equals \(\lim_{\lambda\to 0} v^k_\lambda\), but in compact-continuous games the discounted values may fail to converge (there are counterexamples).

Known sufficient conditions for convergence include certain regularity/tameness assumptions such as \(g\) and \(q\) being separable and definable (in an o-minimal structure), which in particular holds when \(g\) and \(q\) are polynomial functions of the actions. A set \(E\subset\mathbb{R}^d\) is semi-algebraic if it can be described by finitely many polynomial inequalities; a function is semi-algebraic if its graph is semi-algebraic.
problem_definition_latex:
Give necessary and sufficient conditions on the primitives \(I,J,g,q\) of a compact-continuous two-player zero-sum stochastic game with finite state set \(K\) such that, for every initial state \(k\in K\), the limit \(\lim_{\lambda\to 0} v^k_\lambda\) exists (equivalently: characterize when the discounted values converge as \(\lambda\downarrow 0\)), and/or such that the long-run value \(v^k\) exists.

In particular, determine whether convergence (or existence of \(v^k\)) holds when \(I\) and \(J\) are semi-algebraic sets and \(g\) and \(q\) are semi-algebraic functions.

Give necessary and sufficient conditions on the primitives \(I,J,g,q\) of a compact-continuous two-player zero-sum stochastic game with finite state set \(K\) such that, for every initial state \(k\in K\), the limit \(\lim_{\lambda\to 0} v^k_\lambda\) exists (equivalently: characterize when the discounted values converge as \(\lambda\downarrow 0\)), and/or such that the long-run value \(v^k\) exists.

In particular, determine whether convergence (or existence of \(v^k\)) holds when \(I\) and \(J\) are semi-algebraic sets and \(g\) and \(q\) are semi-algebraic functions.
location_in_paper: Section 6.2 (Compact-continuous stochastic games), page 16 (in the provided 19-page text)
open_problem_quote: Determining necessary and sufficient conditions on \(I\), \(J\), \(g\), and \(q\) which ensure the convergence of the discounted values or the existence of the value is a problem. Bolte, Gaubert and Vigeral [5] provided sufficient conditions, namely that \(g\) and \(q\) are separable and definable. Without going into a precise definition of these two conditions, they hold in particular when the pay-off function \(g\) and the transition \(q\) are polynomials in the players’ actions. However, the case where \(I\), \(J\), \(g\), and \(q\) are semi-algebraic is still unsolved.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: To refute proposed sufficient conditions, one can search over parameterized families of compact-continuous games and numerically test convergence of \(v^k_\lambda\) as \(\lambda\downarrow 0\), but the space of possible \(g\) and \(q\) is infinite-dimensional and convergence detection is delicate (requires resolving values accurately for small \(\lambda\)).
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate conditions (e.g., structural restrictions on \(g\) and \(q\)) and candidate counterexamples can be represented programmatically within restricted parametric families (polynomial/semi-algebraic templates), with a fitness signal based on numerical evidence of convergence/nonconvergence of \(v^k_\lambda\) computed via discretization/LP approximations. However, the proxy may be noisy and computationally expensive for very small \(\lambda\), and proving necessity/sufficiency would still require theory beyond search.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
In finite-action zero-sum stochastic games, convergence \(\lim_{\lambda\downarrow 0} v^k_\lambda\) and existence of the uniform (Mertens–Neyman) value hold universally (Bewley–Kohlberg; Mertens–Neyman). The source paper of Attia–Oliu-Barton reframes these results via auxiliary parameterized games \(W^k_\lambda(z)\) built from determinants \(d^0_\lambda,d^k_\lambda\): for each \(\lambda\), \(v^k_\lambda\) is the unique \(z\) with \(\operatorname{val} W^k_\lambda(z)=0\), and in the finite case the Shapley–Snow rationality of matrix-game values yields a rational-in-\(\lambda\) structure near \(0\) that forces the existence of \(F^k(z)=\lim_{\lambda\to 0} \operatorname{val} W^k_\lambda(z)/\lambda^n\), hence convergence.

In compact-continuous games (compact metric actions, continuous \(g,q\)), Attia–Oliu-Barton show Theorem 1 extends (the \(W^k_\lambda(z)\) characterization still holds), but the convergence mechanism breaks: Vigeral constructs a compact-continuous counterexample with no asymptotic value, so no general necessary-and-sufficient characterization is known. The strongest general positive results to date are structural sufficient conditions: Bolte–Gaubert–Vigeral prove convergence when the primitives are “definable” (o-minimal) and satisfy a separability hypothesis, covering many analytic/algebraic data such as polynomial dependence on actions. However, the semi-algebraic case in full generality (without additional separability/tameness beyond semi-algebraicity) remains open as highlighted in the source paper.

Recent work related to the Attia–Oliu-Barton framework develops finer asymptotic/robustness tools rather than a full characterization. In particular, the thesis on robustness of solutions in game theory extends the auxiliary-game approach to marginal (directional-derivative) values in compact-continuous stochastic games for each fixed \(\lambda>0\), and studies limits of these marginal quantities as \(\lambda\to 0\) under boundedness/regularity assumptions. Separately, algorithmic work on partially observable stochastic games provides additional sufficient conditions (ergodic/Doeblin-type) for existence and approximation of uniform values in finite-action hidden/blind models, but does not resolve the perfect-monitoring compact-continuous convergence question. Overall, the open direction is to identify conditions—potentially via definability, stratification, or strengthened regularity of the induced Shapley operator—that recover a finite-type asymptotic description of \(\operatorname{val} W^k_\lambda(z)\) near \(\lambda=0\), thereby yielding convergence in broad semi-algebraic classes.

sources.title: Robustness of solutions in game theory: Values and strategies in partially observable, perturbed, stochastic, and infinite games
sources.theorem_or_result: The thesis (esp. Chapter 4, based on [AOBS25]) proves that for any finite-state zero-sum stochastic game with compact metric action sets and continuous payoff/transition (compact-continuous case) and any admissible perturbation direction, the discounted marginal value \,\(\partial_H v(\Gamma_\lambda)\) exists for each fixed \(\lambda>0\) and is characterized as the unique \(z\in\mathbb R\) such that \[\operatorname{val}_{O^*(\Gamma_\lambda)}\big(\partial_H\Delta^k_\lambda - v(\Gamma_\lambda)\,\partial_H\Delta^0_\lambda - z\,\Delta^0_\lambda\big)=0,\] where \(\Delta^0_\lambda,\Delta^k_\lambda\) are the auxiliary matrices of [AOB19] and \(\operatorname{val}_{O^*(\Gamma_\lambda)}\) is the value of the matrix game restricted to optimal strategies of \(W_\lambda(v(\Gamma_\lambda))\). It also gives a characterization of \(\lim_{\lambda\to 0}\partial_H v(\Gamma_\lambda)\) under boundedness assumptions, and an implicit-function-type formula for the marginal undiscounted value under regularity assumptions.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for necessary and sufficient conditions on compact-continuous stochastic games ensuring convergence of discounted values \(v^k_\lambda\) as \(\lambda\downarrow 0\) (and/or existence of the long-run/uniform value), with special interest in semi-algebraic primitives. This thesis does not provide such a characterization. It instead studies robustness/perturbation and marginal values, and (in Chapter 4) extends marginal-value formulas to the compact-continuous setting for each fixed \(\lambda>0\). While it uses and cites the classical convergence result of Bewley–Kohlberg for finite-action games (via semi-algebraic methods) and discusses compact-continuous extensions, it does not establish new general convergence criteria for \(v^k_\lambda\) as \(\lambda\to 0\) in compact-continuous games, nor does it resolve the semi-algebraic case posed in the open problem. At best, it provides related tools (auxiliary matrix-game representations, perturbation formulas) that could be useful in analyzing convergence in structured classes.

sources.title: Algorithmic Aspects in Partially Observable Stochastic Games
sources.theorem_or_result: The thesis establishes sufficient conditions ensuring existence of long-run/uniform values in certain partially observable stochastic games: - In ergodic blind stochastic games (finite states, finite actions, no signals), the uniform value exists (Theorem 1.2.13) and the approximation problem is decidable (Theorem 1.2.14). - In Doeblin hidden stochastic games (finite states/actions/signals), the uniform value exists (Theorem 1.2.21) and can be approximated algorithmically given the Doeblin parameters (Theorem 1.2.22). It also shows that an ergodicity-type condition in the hidden setting does not guarantee existence of the uniform value (Theorem 1.2.19).
sources.connection: The open problem concerns characterizing when discounted values \(v^k_\lambda\) converge as \(\lambda\downarrow 0\) (and/or existence of long-run value) in compact-continuous zero-sum stochastic games, with special interest in semi-algebraic data. This thesis works mostly in a different regime: finite action sets and, often, partial observation (hidden/blind). Its results provide additional sufficient conditions (ergodicity/Doeblin/primitivity) for existence of the uniform (long-run) value and approximation algorithms in those finite-action partially observable models. These conditions are not necessary-and-sufficient characterizations, and they do not address compact action sets or semi-algebraic continuity assumptions. Thus it does not solve the stated characterization problem, but it contributes related sufficient conditions and counterexamples about when long-run/uniform values fail or hold under information constraints.
